primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected congenital Long QT syndrome (LQT2) presenting with acoustically-triggered convulsive syncope

pathophysiology_summary: Congenital Long QT syndrome (LQTS) is an inherited cardiac channelopathy in which delayed ventricular repolarisation (a prolonged QT interval, QTc typically >470 ms) predisposes to early afterdepolarisations and the polymorphic ventricular tachycardia Torsades de Pointes, which can cause syncope, cardiac arrest and sudden death. Different genotypes have characteristic triggers: LQT1 events are typically exertional (especially swimming), LQT2 events are classically triggered by sudden auditory/emotional stimuli (alarm clocks, telephone ringing), and LQT3 events occur at rest/sleep. Here, syncope triggered immediately by a loud alarm strongly suggests LQT2. The collapse was a convulsive syncope: transient cerebral hypoperfusion during the arrhythmia caused brief hypoxic stiffening, but the rapid complete recovery without tongue-biting or prolonged post-ictal confusion distinguishes it from a primary seizure. A structurally normal heart with a normal resting examination is typical - LQTS is an electrical, not structural, disorder, so a normal exam does not exclude it; diagnosis requires a 12-lead ECG to measure the QTc (with correction for heart rate), continuous monitoring, and specialist/genetic assessment. A family history of young sudden death (the maternal aunt at 25) markedly raises the probability. The immediate danger is a recurrent, potentially fatal Torsades event if the patient is re-triggered - and the acoustic trigger (fire-alarm testing) is still present. Management centres on avoiding triggers and QT-prolonging drugs, urgent ECG/monitoring, and specialist-initiated beta-blockade (with consideration of an ICD). None of this can be done over telehealth, so immediate emergency assessment with a low-stimulus environment is required.

differential.diagnosis: Inherited arrhythmic syncope (Long QT syndrome / channelopathy)
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden collapse triggered by a loud noise with brief stiffening is a red-flag pattern for an arrhythmic (channelopathy) syncope.
Common cause of syncope, but the absence of a prodrome and the instantaneous startle trigger are atypical and more concerning for arrhythmia.
Brief stiffening raises seizure, but rapid full recovery without tongue-biting/post-ictal confusion favours convulsive syncope from an arrhythmia.
Considered for exertional/arrhythmic syncope, but a completely normal cardiac examination argues against significant structural disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat acoustically-triggered syncope with a young sudden-death family history as possible malignant arrhythmia, not a simple faint or seizure.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Long QT syndrome (LQT2) - acoustically-triggered arrhythmic syncope
differential.evidence_basis: Acoustic-startle-triggered syncope (DI-001) + convulsive syncope with rapid recovery (DI-002) + maternal young sudden death (DI-003), with a structurally normal heart, strongly indicate LQT2.
No prodrome and an instantaneous startle trigger with a malignant family history are not vasovagal.
Rapid recovery without tongue-biting or post-ictal confusion, with a clear cardiac trigger, argues against primary seizure.
Normal cardiac examination and pulses argue against significant structural disease (though echo confirms).

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the leading concern is Long QT syndrome; urgent ECG/monitoring, trigger avoidance and specialist assessment are mandatory.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected congenital Long QT syndrome (LQT2) pending 12-lead ECG (QTc) and specialist/genetic assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Cannot be confirmed without a 12-lead ECG (QTc) and monitoring; the correct action is immediate emergency assessment with a low-stimulus environment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a suspected inherited arrhythmia (Long QT) requiring an immediate ambulance, ECG and monitoring with trigger avoidance - never cleared as a benign startle-faint.

red_flags.description: Syncope triggered immediately by a loud acoustic startle, with abrupt collapse and no prodrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: An auditory-startle trigger is a hallmark of LQT2 and indicates an arrhythmic (Torsades) mechanism with a risk of sudden death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Labelled a startle-faint; the patient is re-triggered and suffers a fatal Torsades event.

red_flags.description: Convulsive syncope (brief stiffening) with rapid full recovery, no tongue-biting/post-ictal state
red_flags.clinical_significance: Convulsive syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion is commonly misdiagnosed as epilepsy; the features here favour an arrhythmic cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misdiagnosis as epilepsy delays cardiac assessment of a lethal channelopathy.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden death at a young age (maternal aunt at 25)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Young sudden death in a relative markedly raises the probability of an inherited channelopathy and the patient's own risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to elicit/weight this leads to under-triage of a fatal inherited condition.

red_flags.description: Prolonged QTc (>470 ms) on 12-lead ECG with a structurally normal heart
red_flags.clinical_significance: A prolonged QTc on ECG is the diagnostic hallmark of Long QT syndrome; it requires a 12-lead ECG and cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - ECG is required; the correct action is immediate emergency assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: An instantaneous collapse from a loud noise without prodrome, with a malignant family history, is not vasovagal and must not be dismissed.
Brief hypoxic stiffening can be mistaken for a seizure; rapid recovery without post-ictal features points to an arrhythmic cause needing cardiac assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reassurance from a normal resting exam
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: LQTS is an electrical disorder with a structurally normal heart; a normal exam does not exclude it - an ECG is required.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to remove the ongoing trigger
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The acoustic trigger is still present; not advising a low-stimulus environment risks a fatal recurrent Torsades event before assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG to measure the corrected QT interval (QTc)
Continuous cardiac monitoring to detect arrhythmia/Torsades
Echocardiography to confirm a structurally normal heart
Genetic testing and specialist electrophysiology assessment
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate ambulance (000) transfer to the emergency department for a 12-lead ECG and continuous cardiac monitoring; in the meantime create a strictly low-stimulus environment (silence all alarms, phones and loud notifications) to avoid re-triggering, keep the patient resting, and have the roommate ready to start CPR if she collapses and is unresponsive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this was just a startle-faint, or advising a next-day GP visit without ECG/monitoring and trigger avoidance, risks a fatal recurrent Torsades de Pointes.
